data.title: Clarkson: It's Perhaps A Good Time To Be Contrarian
data.description:
Introduction

Since my last article on Clarkson PLC (OTCPK:CKNHF) was published, the share price has lost approximately 15% of its value. Clarkson also mentioned it now expects the tariffs as announced by the USA will have a negative impact on its financial results, but the company will remain profitable.

Clarkson is trading with CKN on the London Stock Exchange, and with an average daily volume exceeding 100,000 shares per day, it clearly is the most liquid listing to trade in the company’s stock. As Clarkson reports its financial results in GBP, I will use the GBP as base currency throughout this article. At the current share price of approximately 33.35 GBP, the market capitalization of the company is roughly 1B GBP.

This article is meant as an update to previous coverage. Please read the older article to get a better understanding of Clarkson’s business model and its operational divisions. Clarkson’s main division is the shipbroking business, where it helps clients to buy and sell ships but also to find counterparties for charter agreements and lease agreements.

The asset-light business model still results in a strong free cash flow result

Before discussing the current situation and the trading updates, I think it makes sense to take a step back and revisit the 2024 cash flow results, as that will set the tone of what we can expect in a normalized situation (read: in a tariff-light world with relatively stable FX rates).

Clarkson reported a total revenue of approximately 661M GBP in 2024, which represents an increase of just over 3% compared to the preceding year. The COGS and administrative expenses increased at a slightly higher rate, and after also including the acquisition-related costs to the tune of 3.2M GBP in FY 2024, the reported operating profit of 98.5M GBP was approximately 1.5% lower than in 2023. Excluding these acquisition-related expenses, the operating profit would have increased by approximately 2%.

Fortunately, the company also reported a substantial increase in its net interest income as its balance sheet contains a net cash position (Clarkson’s has no financial debt; only lease liabilities and trade payables). This helped to boost the pre-tax income by approximately 3% while the net attributable income increased to 84.9M GBP, for an EPS of 277 pence. That’s an increase of just 1% compared to the FY 2023 EPS due to the higher average tax rate and the impact of a 3M GBP acquisition-related non-recurring expense. Excluding the latter, the EPS would have come in at just under 287 pence, a 4% increase compared to the 275 pence net income in FY 2023.

As I focused on Clarkson’s cash flow performance in the previous article, it only makes sense to have another look at the cash flow generated by Clarkson’s in 2024. As you can see below, the operating cash flow was almost 115M GBP on a reported basis, but this includes a 26M GBP working capital contribution, whereas it does not include the 14.9M GBP in interest and dividends received by Clarkson's, while it also doesn’t include the 12.7M GBP in lease and interest payments.

This means that on an adjusted basis, the operating cash flow was approximately 93M GBP (and this includes the allowance for bonus accruals, as Clarkson’s offers a very bonus-heavy compensation structure).

After also deducting the 7.3M GBP in investments (excluding M&A-related cash outflow), the underlying net free cash flow was approximately 90M GBP, of which roughly 88M GBP was attributable to the common shareholders of Clarkson PLC. This represents just under 290 pence.

Clarkson is proud of its dividend history, as the company has increased its dividend every single year in over 20 years' time. While the current gross yield of 3.3% isn’t spectacular, it still represents a payout ratio of less than 50% of the earnings.

At the end of 2024, Clarkson PLC has 493.5M GBP in cash (and term deposits), whereas the balance sheet does not contain any debt. However, given the bonus-heavy structure of Clarkson's, I feel the 250M GBP in accrued but unpaid bonuses should be considered a financial liability, as those will eventually have to be paid out (either in cash or through the creation of new shares, and while the latter wouldn’t result in a cash outflow, it would have a dilutive impact on the per-share performance). This means that I am using a pro forma net cash position of approximately 243M GBP for approximately 791 pence per share. This calculation excludes the 12.3M GBP pension surplus.

This means the current enterprise value of Clarkson’s is just around 750M GBP.

As part of its AGM update, Clarkson mentioned it now expects a pre-tax profit of 85-95M GBP as the weaker US Dollar also has a negative impact on Clarkson's financial results, which are reported in GBP. Applying an effective tax rate of 22.5% to the midpoint of the guidance implies a net profit of around 70M GBP, of which about 68M GBP would be attributable to the shareholders of Clarkson PLC.

Investment thesis

That implies an EPS of around 220 pence, indicating the stock is currently trading at approximately 15 times earnings. I also consider the FX volatility the main risk related to an investment in Clarkson, as the majority of the company’s revenue is generated in USD while it reports in GBP.

The underlying business (expressed in USD) remains strong (albeit a bit weaker than in the previous few years), and thanks to the very robust balance sheet, the good reputation in the sector, and an asset-light business model, Clarkson PLC is getting increasingly attractive at the current share price. Given the current FX headwinds, a multi-year investment horizon would be recommended.

I currently have no position in Clarkson, but I am keeping very close tabs.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IBM Q2 Earnings Preview: Still A Buy, Just Not Too Much
data.description:
IBM's (NYSE:IBM) Q2 2025 results approach July 23rd. The stock has seen some welcome gains over the past year.

I covered it last September and rated it a Buy at the time. Total returns are up 30% since then. As we approach Q2 for this year, is it reasonable to take the same stance?

The short answer is yes. The long answer is to consider a smaller allocation and readiness for bumps in the road in Q2 earnings updates.

Current Snapshot

Let's focus on the key things that I believe reflect IBM's intrinsic value and its risk factors, and reiterating some of my points from last year, as well as management's takeaways when Q1 results were released.

  • Annual revenue around $62B
  • Free cash flow around $12B (adjusted for financing receivables)
  • $63.3B in debt
  • $17.6B in cash

This debt has maturities staggered across decades, with manageable maturities for the foreseeable future, either to repay or refinance. It's not something I find troublesome, even if a first look appears a bit high.

These financials derive from three main business segments:

  • Software: Automation, Hybrid Cloud, Transaction Processing, Data
  • Consulting: Strategy (for customer use of AI) and Operations (applications for the AI)
  • Infrastructure: Transaction processing platform and servers

These segments primarily serve large, commercial clients, known for using software and related products to help them run businesses, with emphasis on unlocking the power of AI for customers in these segments.

It's different from the IBM of the past, known for its line of personal computers and other hardware. Today's IBM is more software-focused and less personal, dedicated to products that help businesses run better.

Previous Developments and Talking Points

In my thesis last year, AI was still developing story and market opportunity. I expected that the company would continue to see revenue growth, supported by the strong demand for AI in commercial uses.

That proved to be the case throughout 2024, with strong revenue growth and has continued so far into 2025. Management was extremely optimistic during its Q1 2025 earnings call. CFO James Kavanaugh expressed the following:

We talked about the strategic repositioning of our portfolio and our company overall to much more software-centric, platform-centric model. And we laid out a financial investment thesis, we called it our shareholder value creation thesis, that said we would take this business from a no-growth profile to the last three years kind of low single-digit profile to an accelerating revenue growth profile of mid-single-digit-plus, and that's how we guided 2025.

Amid this positive outlook, management was confident about the state of the business and guided for free cash flow of about $13.5B.

Valuation and Outlook

My previous valuation came from the following Discounted Cash Flow calculation:

Using likely FCF at the time, these basic thoughts under-girded my calculation:

  • 10% CAGR for five years and then 5% after didn't require bold assumptions
  • We could even give a multiple down to 10 in case of future headwinds
  • Dividend policy would help ensure returns are received

I think the basic assumptions were sound and that year-over-year results show IBM essentially following along those lines to date.

If we update to include the forward FCF for 2025 and new share count, that raises the intrinsic value to $251 per share. The current market price is a bit higher, but I believe it's similar to the situation I described last time: a mostly fair value for modest growth, with potentially higher growth to be enjoyed for free.

A case can therefore still be made that IBM is a Buy today, but as the risk-reward asymmetry isn't quite as favorable, we need to consider the matters in Q2 that can confirm or worsen the outlook:

  • Lost business from government cuts
  • Impact of tariffs and trade deals
  • How segments will be affected

Again, I want to emphasize that I think Q2 will serve to confirm or worsen the outlook. While management did praise the Trump Administration (in the Q1 earnings call) for an agenda it believes will help the economy, they did discuss the near-term uncertainty for their own business:

In the near-term, uncertainty may cause clients to pause and take a wait and see approach. However, the value of hybrid cloud automation, data sovereignty, and on-premise solutions becomes even more critical in volatile windows. Recent conversations that I've had with clients reflect this view of the current environment. These conversations vary by industry, business, and geography.

While this earnings call occurred shortly after Liberation Day and the turmoil in capital markets for the month of April, recovery of asset values doesn't mean that major businesses (and thus IBM's customers) believe they have everything figured out. In fact, for many businesses, not just IBM, Q2 earnings calls are going to tell us a lot more about how the tariffs, even just the threat of them, impacted their business models and priorities going forward.

For IBM, this translates to how customers choose their capex, in terms of size and where it's spent. Management did hint which segment would likely be hit first:

Consulting is also more susceptible to discretionary pullbacks and DOGE-related initiatives.

In essence, customers can use IBM's core products without also using the consulting service, but it doesn't work the other way. As this is the lowest-margin business, it would be the least damaging to FCF growth. What we will need to gauge is if the other segments' growth can continue, thereby offsetting it.

If, however, hesitation by customers is more major, leading to delays in development of AI assets because the extent of their need is perceived as lower, this would impact the other segments and FCF growth more heavily.

Conclusion

When I wrote about IBM last year, I talked about managing one's risk, should they invest in this business, by having an exit plan. I think an extra step is to take a smaller position than one would have last time, given these risk factors and the less favorable valuation. IBM is on the other side of a recent turnaround and is worth a modest allocation as a bet on AI. A concentrated bet only makes sense with a lower entry price, barring any Q2 disasters.

As long as one sizes appropriately, however, I think IBM has a place in a long-term portfolio.

data.title: Monarch Casino: This Casino Pick Is Handing Out Serious Returns
data.description:
For over a year, I and a few fellow Seeking Alpha analysts have been shouting that Monarch Casino (NASDAQ:MCRI) was an undervalued gem.

Well, this is paying off handsomely.

If we go back to my first article in April 2024, we've already accumulated a total return of almost 55%, while the S&P returned just over 20%.

If you missed that one, we don't have to look far.

In March of this year—just before my thesis's anniversary—I revisited it and told you: Monarch remains the gem outside of Las Vegas.

Since then, the call has returned over 40%, well ahead of the S&P's almost 11%.

In my last piece, I pointed out how Monarch crushed it in Q1 with a double-beat—and it didn't stop there. The company continued gaining share in both Black Hawk and Reno, thanks to its top-notch properties: Atlantis and Monarch Black Hawk.

Not surprisingly, this continued in Q2 FY 2025, with another double-beat to its name.

To give you an idea, they reported EPS of $1.44 (beating Wall St. estimates by at least $0.25, or 21%). Revenue also came in above expectations, reaching $136.91 million ($7.16 million, or 5.5%, above Wall St. expectations).

Like most of my casino follow-ups, this piece is all about telling the Q2 story—what happened in Reno and Black Hawk, how Monarch's segments performed, and of course, where the stock stands now and what I recommend going forward.

Just as a refresher, I had a $95 price target on Monarch.

But after the stock jumped double digits post-earnings (almost 30% at the time of writing this article), the last section of this article becomes especially relevant.

Not only will I raise that price target (they’re outperforming across the board), but I’ll also weigh in on whether it still makes sense to add to your position now that shares are trading above $100s.

Better Than Strip?

It's funny how we can't put things into the same boxes when we look at different companies.

While the Strip is seeing visitor numbers at least low to mid-single digits lower year-over-year, impacting GGR to the same extent, other parts of the country are holding steady.

You can really see this playing out with companies like Boyd Gaming (BYD)—another Top Pick of mine for 2025—and how they're offsetting a softer Las Vegas locals and Strip market with strength in other properties. Same thing with Golden Entertainment (GDEN), where slower visitation at the STRAT was pointed out by Truist just this week.

And then there's Accel Entertainment (ACEL), a younger operator that's showing some early success with the Fairmount project.

Looking specifically at Reno and Black Hawk, we also have some very interesting dynamics in Q2.

Starting with Black Hawk, CO, data from the Colorado Division of Gaming gives us the following outlook for Q2 2025:

  • April: GGR of $70.3 million (based on Black Hawk's 77% share of Colorado's GGR), an increase of at least 1% year-over-year. Placer.AI hotel data tells us that hotel visitation there fell 3.2% year-over-year;
  • May: GGR was $77 million in the quarter, a very strong 1.4% year-over-year increase. Hotels saw a 1.7% year-over-year decline in visits;
  • June: Based on May's trend, we could guess something like a GGR of $77 million again. However, there was a clear slowdown in discretionary spending in other industries, which could indicate a small decline in GGR, although we don't have the data to confirm this. Placer.AI told us that Colorado saw a 0.7% year-over-year drop in hotel visits—smaller than in many states—giving us some indication that the decline may have been smaller than the national average.

All things considered, Black Hawk seemed to be delivering healthy demand. But remember, Monarch is growing at a higher rate than Black Hawk, thanks to a greater convergence of mid- to high-end customers from the Denver and Boulder metropolitan areas.

If you look at the map, you'll see why.

Flying back to Nevada, specifically Reno, part of Washoe County, we have a particularly interesting scenario.

Just take a look at the GGR data provided by the Nevada Gaming Control Board:

  • April: Washoe County saw a GGR of $86.4 million in April, a massive 4.3% year-over-year increase. Data from Placer.AI gave us a similarly strong outlook for Nevada hotels, with visitation increasing 4.6% year-over-year;
  • May: Reversed the previous month's growth, with GGR falling 2.8% year-over-year in Reno, but stable countywide at $90.6 million. Looking at hotels, visitation grew 1.7% year-over-year in Nevada;
  • June: As in Colorado, we don't yet have data to pinpoint a GGR here. Placer.AI data shows a 2% year-over-year decline in hotel visits in Nevada, which could indicate a low-single-digit decline in GGR.

As with Black Hawk, Monarch appears to be performing above its peers (in Reno thanks to its prime location and its connection to the Reno-Sparks Convention Center).

Let's look inside Monarch for a moment and unpack these results.

Monarch Continues to Gain Share

Starting from the beginning, Monarch reported revenue of $136.91 million, as you already know by now. This represented 6.8% year-over-year growth.

If you look back, this was the highest quarterly growth since Q2 FY 2023!

But what drove this? Let's take a look segment by segment:

  • Casino: Was $79.58 million, approximately 58.1% of total revenue (55.3% in Q2 FY 2025) and growing 12.1% year-over-year;
  • Food & Beverage: Totaled $32.19 million in the quarter. They represented 23.5% of total revenue for the quarter (compared to 24.8% in the same quarter last year) and grew only 1% year-over-year;
  • Hotel: Totaled $19.1 million in the quarter and represented 13.9% of total revenue (compared to nearly 16.4% in the same quarter last year). This was the only segment that saw a decline in revenue (likely driven by Colorado if we follow Placer.AI's data for the quarter) of 3.1% year-over-year;
  • Other: Totaled $6 million and grew 7.3% year-over-year. These aren't particularly significant for our analysis, as they comprise fees and other non-core charges.

It's clear that one thing has driven Monarch's growth: its casinos.

Essentially, its operations and renovations over the past few years are paying off. Its properties are definitely busier than other casinos in the region, a clear indication that they are gaining market share by growing faster than the local GGR.

But what about margins?

Well, a huge advantage of this small operator is that its balance sheet is squeaky clean—freeing up tremendous room to increase shareholder returns through dividends and buybacks.

Let's break it down.

Casino activities generated an operating margin of 64.2%, exceeding the same period last year by at least 200 bp.

But that wasn't all.

F&B (29.6% versus 26.2%) also showed a strong improvement, while Hotels (65.6% versus 66.5%) saw headwinds of almost 100 bp year-over-year.

On the bottom line, what matters most is the Casino. This boosted EBITDA from $42.1 million to $48.1 million (an incredible 14.2% year-over-year increase) and EBIT from $29.7 million to $34.9 million (a 17.4% year-over-year increase).

Another tremendous factor that drove EPS, as I mentioned at the beginning of this analysis, was buybacks. At the end of Q2 FY 2025, they had 18.3 million shares outstanding, a reduction of at least 2.1% year-over-year.

But do you know what's most important in this whole story? If you said 'cash generation', you're right. Unfortunately, Monarch hasn't released its OCF, Capex, and, consequently, FCF data.

All we can do is make assumptions.

I estimate quarterly OCF (without considering potential headwinds from operating assets) to be $40 million (considering reported net income of $27 million). With Capex of $13 to $20 million (maximum), we have FCF ranging from $20 to $27 million.

And this is where things get interesting. Monarch have room to do whatever they want with this FCF.

They must already pay an annual dividend of $1.20. So, in the quarter, they have $5.5 million to cover that. That leaves $14.5 to $21.5 million left over for them to do whatever they want.

In recent quarters, they have been accumulating cash ($41.6 million in the three quarters prior to Q2 FY 2025, resulting in $75.1 million in cash at the end of Q1 FY 2025). At the end of Q2 FY 2025, they had $71.6 million, indicating that they may have accelerated buybacks or seen some kind of headwind in operating assets.

In any case, it's an enviable position that allows them to:

  1. Consolidate smaller casinos;
  2. Being sold at a huge premium (combination of synergies, zero debt, sitting on cash and assets that generate a tremendous ROI);
  3. Aggressively return capital to shareholders (further increase dividends and/or buy back their shares more forcefully);
  4. Get into debt.

Monarch is definitely a wild card, and that's exactly what I want to explore with you in the next section.

Buybacks or M&A? I Love It All

To discuss some possible scenarios, I'll choose two: M&A and buybacks.

Starting with the most likely scenario, buybacks. Basically, Monarch continues to milk its shareholders for returns in the form of dividends and buybacks, but it's paying more attention to the latter, as it seems to me the smartest way to do it at this point.

The company has approximately 1.7 million shares in its Buyback Plan (meaning they bought back just over 240,000 shares in the last quarter alone). With 18.3 million shares outstanding, they will be able to wash them down to 16.6 million (a reduction of almost 9.3%, a solid return for shareholders).

But how much would they spend to do so?

Well, if we consider an average purchase price of $100 to $105, we'd have somewhere between $170 and $178.5 million. Assuming they generate FCF of more than $100 million (assuming Capex remains close to $50 million and OCF slightly higher year over year) and $25 million for dividend payments, Monarch would be able to return 9.3% in just over two years.

The return from buybacks would be in the low to mid-single digits year over year. Adding this to the dividend yield of almost 1.5%, we'd have a total annual return very close to 5%.

Not bad at all!

But now, let's talk about something different: M&A. This is something many investment banks, like Truist, love to include in their theses:

Mgmt plans to continue to buyback shares opportunistically. With net leverage at around zero (amongst the best in our coverage) MCRI is well positioned for M&A, share repurchases and/or a special dividend. That said, mgmt noted that the M&A environment remains challenged but will continue to evaluate gaming and even non-gaming targets as well

That said, what would be the premium paid for Monarch if a buyout were to occur?

I believe that for Monarch, a small operator with strong margins, iconic assets, and a pristine balance sheet, I would say an EV/EBITDA multiple of 11x to 12x is quite justifiable. Assuming EBITDA for FY 2025 is $190 to $200 million, we would arrive at an EV of $2.24 billion at the midpoint. Adding cash to this, the Equity Value would be something close to $2.32 billion.

If we consider the current outstanding shares (18.3 million), the acquisition price per share would be $126. This is still a premium of at least 21% at the midpoint.

Is It Still a 'Buy'?

To answer this question, we need to clarify a few things.

First, I expect Monarch to start returning capital to shareholders more aggressively from now on—dividends (perhaps even special dividends), buybacks, and everything else.

But how much?

It's hard to quantify exactly, but I expect something close to 5% annually.

Second, given the company's track record, I don't believe we'll see any M&A activity for now. So, for my base case, I'll stick with organic growth.

Third, and most importantly: should I stick with my $95 price target? Well, probably not.

Once again, we need to update some of our estimates. After all, they're crushing it in operations.

For EBITDA, we'll estimate double-digit growth (something between 10 and 15% seems appropriate here), reaching between $190 and $200 million. EBIT will grow at the same pace, reaching between $133 and $140 million.

If we use an EV/EBITDA of 9x (which I consider valid for the organic growth thesis due to the debt-free balance sheet and asset quality), which represents a 10% to 15% premium compared to other operators, we would obtain a price target of $100 to $105, depending on the EBITDA we use.

Using the same parameters for EV/EBIT (a 13x multiple, already at a premium compared to other operators trading at 11x or 12x), we would arrive at a price target of $101 to $106.

Interesting where we ended up. Precisely where the stock price rested today after the rally.

Adding all this up, I'm left with a price target very close to $105 and reiterating my 'Buy' rating, not because of the upside, but because of the low-to-mid single-digit annual returns and a number of options with the cash they're sitting on.

As I said, if they want, Monarch could achieve double-digit annual returns by aggressively repurchasing its shares later this year using cash on hand, or they could do so with their FCF and continue accumulating for a future opportunity to consolidate a smaller player without even using debt.

Ultimately, Monarch continues to gain share in a soft market—which tells us that once things heat up, the organic growth thesis could even accelerate.

data.title: Meta's Playbook Of Scale First, Monetize Later Will Continue With WhatsApp And AI
data.description:
Intro

The sun was shining across the first baseline at Dodger Stadium. As Shohei Ohtani stepped up to the plate, the crowd roared; the thumping bass of Kendrick’s Not Like Us felt more relevant than ever, juxtaposed between super-star athletes and the recent headlines on the salary offers of AI researchers.

Ohtani made headlines in 2023 after signing a record-breaking $700mm contract with the Los Angeles Dodgers. The deal, which spans 10 years, was one of the largest contracts in professional sports history at the time. Leaving behind the Los Angeles Angels and their superstar Mike Trout, it symbolized the power that money could have in coaxing talent. Sitting in that stadium, the game set the backdrop for an even stranger bidding war happening over the past few weeks: the race for AI talent.

As a team of AI investors, the headlines around Meta were hard to avoid. But the biggest reason we’ve been mulling over this headhunting battle was the particular news of a figurehead in the industry, whose company we were considering investing in, joining Meta after a massive funding round for his own company.

At the same time, we felt a sense of comfort, knowing that Mark Zuckerberg is continuing to share his vision for the future, which in turn is reverberating with top Al researchers from across Silicon Valley. As such, we wanted to publish our thoughts around Meta, bringing in several years of research we have been compiling since 2022.

Zuckerberg's Foresight

Zuckerberg’s ability to see the future, previously demonstrated by his early investment in GPU clusters, is now being applied to make AI a core part of Meta.

To a question from Dwarkesh Patel's 2024 podcast interview on how he knew to purchase H100s back in 2022, Mark Zuckerg answered as follows (emphasis our own):

I think it was because we were working on Reels. We always want to have enough capacity to build something that we can't quite see on the horizon yet… We were constrained on the infrastructure in catching up to what TikTok was doing as quickly as we wanted to. I basically looked at that and I was like “hey, we have to make sure that we're never in this situation again. So let's order enough GPUs to do what we need to do on Reels and ranking content and feed. But let's also double that.” Again, our normal principle is that there's going to be something on the horizon that we can't see yet.

This aggressive investment in infrastructure is attracting top AI researchers today and is reinforcing a clear vision he has for the future. While the market scrutinizes the heavy capital expenditure, we see a familiar pattern of strategic investment poised to unlock value.

We believe significant upside will be driven by the under-monetized WhatsApp platform, with emerging opportunities in cloud API services and agentic AI providing further growth catalysts. These are the types of initiatives that only founder-led and controlled companies can undertake, and we believe Mark Zuckerberg is best of breed.

Q1 2025 Earnings Recap

Meta’s 1Q25 results exceeded guidance across key metrics, reaching $42.3B in revenue and surpassing the high end of the company's forecast range ($39.5B to $41.8B). This represented a 16% YoY growth. Meta’s Family of Apps drove $41.9B, with its Reality Labs division accounting for the rest.

Operating income came in at $17.6B, representing a 41% operating margin. Net income was $16.6B, representing a 39% net income margin.

User growth continued to be the highlight, with over 3.35B people now using at least one Meta app daily. This is half of the world’s population ex-China.

Employee count expanded to over 76,800 employees, indicating an inflection point and the end of the “Year of Efficiency” for Meta after having reduced employee count from 86k in 2022 to 67k in 2023.

Meta’s ARPP (average revenue per people) number continues to impress, reaching $12.36 in 1Q25, a 4% YoY increase vs. $11.89 in 1Q24.

This strong operational foundation is enabling the company to embark on an aggressive strategic expansion into AI, marked by a substantial increase in capital expenditure and stock-based compensation for top talent.

Not surprisingly, the earnings call was largely dominated by discussions of Meta's AI strategy, with CEO Mark Zuckerberg emphasizing that "AI is transforming everything we do". Management outlined five major AI opportunities the company is pursuing (AI in business, engaging experiences, business messaging, Meta AI, and devices), describing them as long-term investments downstream from building general intelligence and leading AI models.

A notable announcement was the substantial increase in Meta's 2025 capital expenditure guidance, updated to $64B to $72B from previous guidance of $60B to $65B. This increase is primarily driven by data center investments and GPU purchases. Management also highlighted that demand for compute resources across the company is outpacing supply, with teams experiencing bottlenecks in AI capacity.

The stock price action after the April 30th earnings announcement was somewhat muddied by the lingering tension around tariffs. Nonetheless, the stock popped 4.2% the day after to end the next day at $572.21 per share.

What Moves Meta’s Stock?

Looking back at the price action of Meta, investor sentiment and response to earnings primarily depend on:

  • CAPEX commitments and forecasts
  • Revenue forecasts
  • Growth trajectories of Meta's Family of Apps, such as Reels, Threads, and WhatsApp

Considering the initiatives that Meta is taking, we believe there are several monetization opportunities to continue fueling their revenue.

Investors should, however, take note that some of these initiatives may take several quarters to a few years to take off, as seen with previous efforts.

For example, Meta's click-to-message platform quickly became one of the company’s most impressive growth engines. Originally launched in 2015 to increase the direct interaction with businesses, business messaging took off by late 2019, becoming Meta’s fastest-growing ad product.

Even Mark Zuckerberg was caught off guard.

Despite years of limited shared information, Meta revealed in late 2022 that click-to-message had reached a $9B annual run rate, including $1.5B from WhatsApp, which had grown 80% YoY. By Q4 2022, the total platform revenue of click-to-message had grown to about $2.5B, putting it on pace to more than double Snapchat’s annual revenue.

Today, over 1B users message businesses weekly, and more than 40% of advertisers use the format. Over half of those are doing so exclusively, indicating strong incremental demand.

This captures the essence of how Mark Zuckerberg works. He experiments iteratively to see what works. He then makes a big reveal about the impact of these experiments once they reach a sizeable scale.

We observed a similar phenomenon with Reels.

Meta introduced Instagram Reels in August 2020 as a strategic response to TikTok's rapid growth in the short-form video market. It then integrated it into Facebook in 2022.

Reels has achieved substantial user growth since its launch. In May 2024, it reached 1.7B monthly active users. In 1Q24, Reels time spent accounted for 50% of Instagram's total time spent, and last year, it reached a new milestone of 4.5B reshares each day.

On Instagram, Reels and video continue to drive engagement, with Reels alone now making up 50% of the time that's spent within the app. Similarly, Reels makes up one-third of the time spent watching videos on Facebook.

Along these lines, Mark Zuckerberg is finally turning to WhatsApp to monetize on its 3B monthly active users.

WhatsApp: A Core Driver of Future Upside

Before Meta’s acquisition in 2014, WhatsApp was free in the first year but charged a $0.99 annual fee thereafter. Users got access to features like group chat, audio and video file sharing, and location sharing, something that traditional SMS/MMS did not have. This charge was phased out shortly after the acquisition in 2016 to allow its user base to grow unrestricted.

WhatsApp Business Platform was launched in 2018 as a way to monetize from businesses using the app. It allows businesses to communicate with customers through API-based solutions, running under a freemium model of 1,000 free conversations before charging a fee. WhatsApp also generates substantial revenue through the aforementioned click-to-message ads. These appear on Facebook and Instagram but direct users to WhatsApp conversations.

WhatsApp's monetization progress has become increasingly important in recent years, as seen with the WhatsApp Business Platform revenue growth being a significant contributor to the 34% increase in Family of Apps' other revenue in Q1 2025.

Even with the success of these monetization efforts in the past few years, we believe there are two additional core opportunities in the short- to medium-term.

AI Integration for Businesses

Customer service is mainly a scaling issue on both ends.

With a small user base, addressing customer needs across phone, email, and chat is manageable with a customer service team. But once the customer number grows, it becomes more and more difficult. On the other hand, if a user base is too small, it’s often hard to justify spending the money to create a customer service team, leading to a terrible customer experience.

Meta’s AI-powered customer service bot, also known as Business AI, solves both of these issues. A large company with a diverse set of products and customer base can incorporate custom AI models trained on the company’s data to address customer needs. Humans can be in the loop to address concerns that are not resolved. Similarly, smaller companies that may not be able to justify a customer service team could have a simple chatbot that can resolve basic questions, vastly improving the customer service experience.

With a single phone call costing $5 to $20 due to labor cost, the upside potential on the customer service side is significant, especially when combined with the click-to-message ads that Meta provides

An additional monetization area is agents. Meta hired Salesforce AI CEO Clara Shih to help build out its B2B business. From our discussion with a few agent startups, Meta is collaborating with these firms to integrate custom solutions for business customers. These startups get insider access to the Llama team while Meta gets an integration partner, similar to Accenture.

Agents are getting smarter and smarter, with companies like Cloudflare creating and supporting the pipelines to enable new use cases. These agents, similar to customer service chatbots, are allowing smaller businesses to get more done with less, whether that’s filling out paperwork or automating email responses.

Meta also started offering Llama API services as a way to allow customers to experience Llama as it was designed, albeit to a limited set of customers. Due to the open-source nature of the models, users were using them on their own devices, running them on third-party clouds, or using API endpoints with unknown quantization levels. With Meta offering these APIs on their cloud, Meta will be able to provide a vertically integrated service that is much cheaper while also allowing its CAPEX spend to be justified by increasing the utilization rate of its GPUs.

While these initiatives are still early, with Mark Zuckerberg providing minimal information, we believe this broader push into the B2B space could complement Meta’s consumer focus.

WhatsApp Advertisement

After reaching 3B MAUs, the company is attempting to monetize on its user base once again.

Advertisement has been Meta’s bread and butter since the beginning, and they’re finally pushing it on WhatsApp. Advertisements will only appear on the Updates Tab and not on the Messages Tab.

We believe a good comp is Snapchat, which has a similar approach of advertising on a messaging app.

Snapchat generated $2.96 in ARPU in 1Q25, a decline from $3.44 in 4Q24.

Assuming roughly a quarter of SNAP’s ARPU as of 1Q25 implies a full-year ARPU of about $1.50. With about 1.5B in DAU on WhatsApp, we assume a conservative $2.25B in revenue, or about a 1.3% incremental increase from its $164.5B FY24 revenue in the initial phases of its implementation.

There’s further upside, as we see Meta easily matching the ARPU number of Snapchat with its improved recommendation system. A $3 to $4.50 ARPU figure leads to a revenue of $4.5B and $6.75B in the coming years, getting the company closer to its $200B revenue milestone.

Assuming a roughly 40% net income margin, for this business at the $6.75B estimated revenue, and a roughly 27-28x multiple given its $25.64 TTM EPS and $717.51 stock price, we arrive at roughly $746.21 just from the incremental WhatsApp advertisement business alone.

At a higher level, given the $200B runrate we expect Meta to reach within the next 2 years, we expect Meta to easily reach $900 per share at 28x multiple.

Risk to the Thesis

While there is potential for Meta to strike out on its efforts on WhatsApp, especially as it relates to the willingness for businesses to spend money or the impressions that the ads on WhatsApp may generate, we believe there are many different avenues that Meta could take to expand its monetization efforts, especially Reels and Threads. As such, most of the long-term risk as it relates to monetization is de-risked.

In the short term, however, the biggest risk we see is tariff-related sentiment across advertisers and over expenditure on CAPEX and talent.

As it relates to tariffs, however, sentiments appear to have shifted in recent weeks from the initial negative reaction seen in April.

Per FundamentalBottom:

While the fickleness of advertisers' and their willingness to spend may be an issue, well-positioned companies typically continue to spend money on effective advertisement channels.

Per Bloomberg:

It’s “counterintuitive” to cut advertising in a time of economic stress because that’s exactly the time when it’s good to market to consumers that are being more strict with their budget, according to Bernstein’s Maas. Advertising did tend to be cut first in past recessions, which hurt the likes of Publicis, IPG and Omnicom.

Similar approaches were seen with Temu, who, a couple weeks ago, reversed its ad spending cuts implemented shortly after tariffs were announced.

The second potential risk we see is as it relates to a surprise uptick in CAPEX and stock-based compensation. Meta has been rapidly expanding its CAPEX, claiming to be building a multi-GW cluster.

At the same time, it is poaching talent from OpenAI, DeepMind, and xAI, driving up stock-based compensation. These expenses, across depreciation, interest expense, and stock-based compensation, could have a downward pressure on the company's margins ~40% net income margin. The expenses around data center investment and operations can be massive, as we laid out in our CoreWeave article a few months back.

Nonetheless, we believe this is the right move for Meta. The company has a much lower cost of capital. It also builds out its own datacenters to make it a core part of its infrastructure, not a one off investment to account for overage in demand both internally and externally. From another one of our articles analyzing CoreWeave.

Our recent attendance at the MLSys Conference highlighted the significant effort Meta is investing in their data center design to enable not just internode and inter-server optimizations, but also inter-datacenter optimization to train increasingly larger models. This requires reimagining the data center from the ground up to reduce communication overhead and compute coordination needs. Consequently, CoreWeave's data centers are not receiving the same attention that first-party data centers are.

Similar to what we saw in 2022, this spend will allow Meta to improve its infrastructure to support its efforts around AI but also for future innovations that will emerge in the next few years.

The key risk is an overreaction from the market, as seen in 2022 when margins notably contracted.

Q2 2025 Earnings Preview

As we look ahead to Q2 2025 earnings, revenue is expected to be in the range of $42.5B and $45.5B. This implies a 9% to 16% jump YoY, which would be below the 22% jump seen in Q2 2024. However, given the fact that this estimate was given when sentiment was extremely negative, we expect a number closer to 16%, with a high possibility of a beat. What we are focusing on are possible signals that spending reduction has bounced back with minimal impact from tariffs, which we expect to be received positively by the market.

Closing Thoughts

Meta is our top pick for long-term shareholders. The company's formidable free cash flow, currently $36B annually, provides a war chest to fund experimentation and acquire the best AI talent on the planet. Mark Zuckerberg's proven, founder-led strategy of scaling products before aggressively monetizing them is now being directed at the massive opportunity in AI and WhatsApp.

We believe the market is underappreciating this potential and that current investments will pave the way for the next phase of significant growth and shareholder value creation. We advise readers to join us on this exciting ride.

data.title: Alphabet: This 'Threat' Could Actually Be A Blessing
data.description:
According to a recent report, two new AI-powered browsers are about to hit the market, one from OpenAI and the other from Perplexity. The news attracted a lot of attention and speculation. While there’s not much information about the browsers just yet, some are considering these browsers a potential threat to Alphabet Inc.’s (NASDAQ:GOOG, NASDAQ:GOOGL) search revenue.

As an Alphabet bull, I feel like I need to confirm this myself, especially since my recent ratings on the stock were all a “Strong Buy”. So today, I dove deep past the headlines to see the actual situation of Google’s search revenue and the potential implications of new browsers for Alphabet.

Spoiler: I’m still rating Alphabet a Strong Buy.

Why Are New Browsers a Threat to Chrome?

Google Chrome holds more than two-thirds of the global browser market. According to recent data, 66.58% of the market share belongs to Chrome, which is equivalent to 3.45 billion users around the world—it has a massive user base. I, myself, am writing this analysis on a Chrome browser on my MacBook.

So why are these new kids on the block considered a threat to the biggest player in the neighborhood?

Source: Statcounter.

According to reports, the OpenAI browser (possibly named Aura) is said to be designed to keep user interactions within a ChatGPT-like interface. Therefore, users will no longer need to click through websites, which directly impacts the core of Google’s business model.

You see, Chrome serves as a gateway for Google to collect data from users, which is then fed into Alphabet’s advertising system. It’s also responsible for routing search traffic to Google’s search engine by default.

That mousetrap has been operating for nearly two decades and has generated the vast majority of Alphabet’s revenue—for Q1‘25 alone, for example, Google advertising accounted for $66.89 billion of their $90.23 billion revenue, and Search alone contributed $50.7 billion.

So, how exactly is OpenAI’s browser threatening? Investors are worried about ad spend. If people use a new browser and by extension, a new search engine, Alphabet's ad revenue could be significantly cut.

This new browser is said to integrate tools like OpenAI’s Operator (an AI agent) to automate digital errands on the browser. Those could be auto-filling forms or summarizing content in real-time. It also helps with fetching answers from websites; users won’t need to click through pages and get bogged down by ads; they’ll get direct answers within the browser itself.

OpenAI also has 500 million weekly active ChatGPT users. If OpenAI somehow finds a way for the majority of these users to use the new browser, that would erode Chrome’s market share by around 14%. As a result, it would potentially bring Alphabet’s Search revenue down from $50 billion to somewhere around $36 billion, and it would be the biggest revenue drop in Alphabet's history.

Well, the numbers are a little "absolute", but you get the idea. Even if Chrome were to lose just 10% of its market share, it would translate to a notable decline on Alphabet’s bottom line. Moreover, OpenAI recruited top talent who once worked on building Chrome, including one of Google’s longtime vice presidents. Now, OpenAI could be really onto something.

From a different angle, this new browser will give OpenAI access to user data, which is something they’ve always wanted. It's worth knowing that Alphabet refused to sell off Chrome despite regulatory pressures, and as far as I can remember, they refused to share data with competitors in the past, including OpenAI. And this is the #1 reason for Alphabet’s current regulatory hurdles: an alleged monopoly. Should this new browser succeed, OpenAI wouldn’t need Alphabet’s user data as much as they do now.

On the subject of "should this new browser succeed", I think it will mitigate Alphabet's regulatory pressures from not having an actual competitor.

Safari holds the next biggest market share after Chrome, but the gap between the two is far too immense for Safari to be considered a direct competitor.

As I pointed out in my previous Alphabet analysis, these antitrust issues are the biggest drivers of GOOG/GOOGL stock’s bearish sentiments, though the risks seem to be declining as investors get that the fundamentals are solid and the financials are healthy. So, if these antitrust issues get resolved, I expect the stock to soar.

Also, last week, Perplexity announced they too are entering themselves as players in the browser wars with a product called Comet. However, unlike Chrome, Safari, or OpenAI’s new product, Comet is said to connect with applications like Slack while also automating tasks. So, it seems to me that on the surface, Comet could become something larger than a browser, and it remains to be seen what the final product does.

The Market Could Just Be Over-Extrapolating

Now, before we start writing Chrome’s obituary, let’s pause for a moment.

The market has a ridiculously high tendency to overreact to news, especially when it comes to AI. Do you recall when everyone thought ChatGPT would put an end to Google Search overnight? Surprise, it didn't—Google Search revenue has been consistently growing over the years without signs of slowing down.

As you can see from my chart above, the segment’s annual revenue has increased by around 90% to $198 billion between FY‘20 and FY‘24—a CAGR of 17.4%. Moreover, Alphabet’s search and advertising units are still reporting strong momentum despite all the AI doom and gloom.

But to those wondering if I missed the point on browsers, you need to consider that getting users to change browsers will be far more difficult than changing the search engine.

Again, Chrome is being used by more than two-thirds of the global internet users, and most of us use Chrome as it's deeply integrated into our digital activities, not only because “it’s there.”

For example, our synced passwords, bookmarks, browsing history, logins, and preferences across devices all have to be in one browser. Breaking this chain is like asking someone who prefers homemade coffee to permanently switch to Starbucks (SBUX); it’s not going to happen without a compelling reason.

Moreover, it’s not a secret that Alphabet paid Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) $8 billion to make its apps "default" on Samsung smartphones, which, of course, includes the Chrome browser. And guess what? Samsung currently holds the second-largest share in the global smartphone market.

While there’s a real possibility for OpenAI to offer Samsung and others a partnership to make its browser a default, most Android phones today are already running on Google Services, and any change at the enterprise level is sure to take time—and also create potential lawsuits. And although nothing is certain, I don’t think Apple (AAPL) would ever agree with the idea of setting a 3rd party browser as the default when they default to their very own Safari browser.

More importantly, if you want an AI-powered browser, Chrome already has it. Google provides AI overviews for most of our queries and provides citations to the claims, as you might’ve noticed with your recent searches. It’s a relatively new integration, but it’s now reportedly being used by over 1.5 billion users each month. So, AI browsers aren’t entirely a "new" thing for most users.

Although I must admit that this AI feature isn’t the best for hyper-specific queries, there’s an option to turn it off and proceed with traditional searches. And guess what? There’s a huge demographic that prefers the traditional search method, where they browse websites to gather information, and this group of users is likely to be hesitant about using OpenAI’s AI browser.

Another thing to take note of is that OpenAI’s new browser will be built based on Chromium, which is Alphabet’s open-source browser code; Microsoft Edge and Opera are also built on top of Chromium. So even if OpenAI gains market share, they’d still be building on Google’s foundation. Sure, they might take some customers, but likely won’t “reimagine” the browsing market like many bears think.

Plus, let’s not forget, Alphabet has something that most AI companies don’t have: a heck of a lot of cash, and it continues to grow. Its annual net income in 2024 alone rose 36% to $100.11 billion, which means they have the means to aggressively mitigate market threats—either to outspend OpenAI on marketing or continue pouring billions of dollars into AI development.

Recent GOOGL Stock Performance

Since my last Alphabet analysis, the stock is up nearly 10%, double the broad market's return.

Over the past year, GOOGL stock has traded between $140.53 and $207.05. At the time of publication, GOOGL trades at around $183.58. Although the stock is up just 1.41% over the past year, it remains remarkably stable compared to the broader market over the same period.

Now, if you look closely at the recent price movement, the stock appears to be in a momentum of recovery. GOOGL is now just 12% below its 52-week high of $207. Once the stock clears the psychological $190 level, $200 will be in sight, and barring anything negative, the stock should finally start to trade in line with the rest of its Magnificent 7 peers as Alphabet’s real potential isn’t baked into the price. Actually, Alphabet is the cheapest of the MAG7 based on a P/E GAAP (TTM) metric, which is around 20.42x (orange line).

For a better view, I’ve extracted the P/E GAAP TTM data of the peer group.

Magnificent 7

Among the MAG7 lineup, Alphabet trades at the lowest valuation compared to the broader sector, and if you understand how well the company has performed over the past 5 years, the potential undervaluation becomes more profound.

If we zoom out 7 years, here’s a clear picture of Alphabet’s upward financial trajectory.

Bottom line: if GOOGL stock traded at Nvidia’s valuation, GOOGL’s stock price would be north of $490. If it traded at Tesla’s valuation, the stock would be almost $1,600. It’s only a matter of time before investors wake up and realize that Alphabet isn’t just about browser ad revenue. There’s Google Cloud, Gemini, Apps, and a more recent catalyst: Waymo.

Potential Growth Driver: Waymo

As covered, it’s hard for me to see OpenAI’s new browser (or even Perplexity’s) as a threat to Alphabet, so I’ll shift my focus to the company’s potential growth drivers. This is because, after all, we’re here to make profits, and one of those drivers is Waymo, a self-driving car company.

Waymo is now capable of offering 250,000 paid rides per week, which is equivalent to 1 million miles of travel every month. Should the numbers stick, that’s over a million rides per month. According to reports, Waymo’s revenue increased from $2 million in 2022 to $125 million in 2024—insane growth for a relatively new business in a relatively new market.

Personally, Waymo’s expansion rate is what made it a catalyst that sticks out from the pack, as Google Cloud and Gemini have been well covered. Waymo has also been accelerating its robotaxi rollout and has announced 2026 plans to expand outside its current markets to Atlanta, Miami, and Washington, DC. Moreover, Waymo has begun testing its autonomous vehicles in Japan under a partnership with one of the country’s most-used taxi apps, GO. This also marks the very first Alphabet’s robotaxi to have been driven on a public road outside the U.S.

I was also curious about how Waymo is scaling its operations. The company is expanding rapidly, and to support the expansion, it has partnered with Magna International (MGA) to build a 239,000-square-foot facility, which is expected to produce thousands of Jaguar I-PACE vehicles with Waymo’s AV systems. With this, Waymo is ready for mass deployment. And while Tesla bulls are still waiting for the company to take over the robotaxi market, Alphabet’s Waymo is already delivering.

Right now, Waymo dominates the self-driving vehicle market in the U.S., and with this new facility, we can expect the gap between Waymo and its competitors to widen even further.

My Verdict

I’m reaffirming my Strong Buy rating on Alphabet despite NBN (new browser noise) ahead of the Q2 earnings on July 23rd. More importantly, Alphabet’s Chrome holds the vast majority of the market share, and there are really no visible signs of erosion despite the intense competition brought by large language models, or LLMs.

Alphabet’s numbers are still on a very clear, upward trajectory. The only thing holding it back is the antitrust matters, which I believe will likely be resolved, especially with U.S. President Donald Trump’s business-first government.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Norwegian Cruise Line: Deep-Value Buy Thesis Remains Compelling - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
NCLH Remains Attractively Valued Despite Its Inherent Underperformance - Buy Its Ongoing Recovery

We previously covered Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) in September 2023, discussing how the management had shown great competency in managing the elevated interest rate risks, attributed to the debt swap to mostly fixed rates by FQ2'23.

Combined with the robust cruise booking trends surpassing pre-COVID levels, the excellent advanced ticket sales, and the increasingly booked position, we had initiated a buy rating then, with the caveat that the stock was only suitable for investors who were patient, attributed to its uncertain path to pre-pandemic profitability.

NCLH 1Y Stock Price

Since then, NCLH has had a robust outperformance at +36.2%, albeit underperforming the wider market at +44.6% and many of its cruise/travel peers in varying degrees.

The cruise company's performance reversal has been prolonged as previously posited, as observed in the management's relatively lacking reiterated FY2025 guidance with net yields of +2.5% YoY, adj EBITDA margin of 37% (+11.3 points YoY/+7.4 from FY2019 levels of 29.6%), and adj EPS of $2.05 (+12.6% YoY/-59.7% from FY2019 levels of $5.09).

Much of NCLH's headwinds are naturally attributed to the still hefty net debt load at -$13.8B (-6.9% QoQ/-5.3% YoY/-111% from FY2019 levels of -$6.54B) and the elevated net-debt-to-adj-EBITDA ratio of 5.66x (compared to 5.27x a quarter ago, 6.3x a year ago, and 3.4x in FY2019).

When compared its direct peers, including Carnival Corporation (CCL) at a net-debt-to-adj-EBITDA ratio of 3.7x in FQ2'25 and Royal Caribbean Group (RCL) at 3.39x in FQ1'25, it is apparent that NCLH's balance sheet recovery has been slower as expected, against the latter's FY2026 "target of reaching the mid-4s."

Much of the debt headwinds are attributed to NCLH's ongoing fleet renewal and capacity growth, with the management aiming to grow from 19.2M passengers in 2019 to 23.4M passengers in 2024 and eventually to 29.7M passengers by 2028, through numerous new ships.

While we have observed a similar fleet capacity growth cadence in both CCL and RCL, along with the increased investments in private island destinations, it is undeniable that NCLH's progress may have been overly aggressive, as observed in the slower balance sheet recovery.

The same has been observed in NCLH's relatively lower Net Yield growth at $279.51 in FQ1'25 (+1.1% YoY/+8.6% from FY2019 levels of $257.20), against CCL at $200.07 in FQ2'25 (+6.4% YoY/+12% from FY2019 levels of $178.59) and RCL at $258.83 in FQ1'25 (+4.7% YoY/+23.9% from FY2019 levels of $208.88).

As a result of these mixed numbers, we can understand why NCLH's stock price performance has underwhelmed indeed, with its reversal to pre-pandemic levels likely to be prolonged.

With the cruise company expected to report their FQ2'25 earnings call on July 31, 2025, investors may want to closely monitor their performance indeed, especially attributed to the relatively underwhelming consumer deposits of $3.9B in FQ1'25 (+2.6% YoY) compared to CCL at $8.5B (+2.4% YoY) and RCL at $6.33B (+4.8% YoY), as NCLH also reports a notable "softening in its 12-month forward booked position."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates have been consistently downgraded, with NCLH expected to chart an impacted top/bottom-line growth profile at a CAGR of +8.2%/+16.5% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +9.7%/+20.7%, the 5Y historical performance at +8%/-18.6%, and the 5Y pre-pandemic performance at +15.6%/+17.5%, respectively.

NCLH Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has continued to discount NCLH's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 11.21x, compared to its 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 15.44x, the sector median of 17.22x, CCL at 14.51x, and RCL at 22.15x.

Even so, thanks to its cheap FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.21x and the excellent consensus adj EPS 3Y growth estimates at a CAGR of +16.5%, it is undeniable that NCLH remains compelling at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.67x, compared to the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 0.88x, the sector median of 1.81x, CCL at 0.65x, and RCL at 1.08x.

This development implies that NCLH's investment thesis remains highly attractive, with it offering value-oriented investors the chance to invest in the cruise sector, naturally for those who have missed CCL's and RCL's massive stock gains thus far.

So, Is NCLH Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

NCLH 5Y Stock Price

For now, NCLH remains well supported in its uptrend recovery line since early 2023, with the stock consistently generating higher highs/higher lows albeit yet to climb out of its COVID-19 rout after the early 2020 meltdown.

NCLH's Seemingly Overbought Levels

Despite NCLH's seemingly overbought levels, it appears that the rally may still have legs based on the historical trends and technical indicators above, with a breakout from current ranges of $23s likely.

Based on the LTM adj EPS of $1.72 (+65.3% sequentially) and the currently discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 11.21x, it seems that the stock is trading at a slight premium to our base-case fair-value estimates of $19.20.

Even so, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $2.88, we believe that NCLH continues to offer an excellent upside potential of +39.6% to our base-case long-term price target of $32.20.

Assuming an eventual re-rating nearer to its 5Y pre-pandemic mean of ~15x (nearer to the sector median of ~17x), there remains a near doubling upside potential to our bull-case long-term price target of $43.20 as well.

Combined with the bullish uptrend support line and the promising technical indicators, we are cautiously reiterating our Buy rating for NCLH, with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer before adding, preferably after a moderate retracement to the 50/100 day moving averages of $19s for an improved margin of safety.

Given the -17% downside from current levels, we reckon some patience may be more prudent in the mean time.

data.title: Netflix Stock: Q2 Earnings Were Much Better Than The Market Thinks
data.description:
The latest earnings report out of Netflix, Inc. (NASDAQ:NFLX) has created surprisingly mixed feelings among analysts, despite a boost to full-year revenue and profit projections and beats on the top and bottom line.

It Wasn't Just The Dollar, It Was The Dollars

The funny thing is that I’ve been somewhat more skeptical about Netflix than the prevailing market consensus for the last year or so - and that has definitely caused me to miss out on some profits on Netflix shares - but I was actually more impressed by the most recent report than the market was. Netflix shares actually fell 1% following the report, while I felt better about Netflix than I had in a while.

The market’s concerns, almost certainly, stem from the currency issue. Historically, the market has quite rightly declined to credit companies for improvements in financials that stem from depreciation of the dollar; such boost to profit are not reflective of any operational improvement by management and have the shelf life of whatever force is driving the dollar down.

But while it is true that Netflix did see some benefit last quarter from the decline of the dollar, that is hardly all that was happening in the report. Indeed, the growth of the UCAN region, about as dollar-independent as you can get, was an almost stunning 15%, returning to the rate of the holiday quarter and far exceeding the 9% of the prior quarter.

The dollar also is not falling everywhere. The single largest impact of the F/X rate was in Latin America, where 9% growth was actually 23% in constant-dollar currency. While that’s not quite enough to cancel out the F/X impact from Europe and Asia, the dollar depreciation boost is far from the only thing driving Netflix revenues up.

Subscriber Growth Seems Clear

I have my own concerns about Netflix, and this report did not fully address them, but I have to say the quarter impressed me. I had thought that, with Paid Sharing already more or less fully penetrated into the base, advertising still a work in progress at best, and the increasing diversion of funds to expensive sports rights instead of broad general entertainment categories, Netflix would be hitting a few bumps in the road by now. So far it isn’t showing up in the data.

Netflix no longer discloses subscriber counts, but it seems almost impossible that it would be hitting these kinds of marks if it wasn’t still attracting significant new subscribers domestically, as well as abroad. Following on the heels of Netflix’s rather large price hike, the retention really has been quite remarkable.

Content Isn't King....But It Sure Helps

Management reaffirmed what it has said many times before, that even their top titles represent less than 1% of total viewing. Netflix Originals notwithstanding, in business terms the company is a platform, not a traditional content creator, by which I mean that the latter does not move the needle nearly like the former does. For this reason, I don’t really see the need to call out the top titles in a given quarter; Netflix is so successful precisely because when one title loses its freshness another is ready to replace it.

Still, there were a few things that might augur well for Netflix’s future content initiatives. First, one of their top titles this past quarter was KPop Demon Hunters, which is an animated film. This comes only a few years after Netflix animation was so down in the dumps that several of its top animation talents were debating jumping ship. One film does not make a trend, but if Netflix can bounce back consistently in this area it will only further grow its appeal to subscribers of all ages.

Secondly, it was noteworthy what programming wasn’t on Netflix’s quarterly top hits list. Specifically, Stranger Things Season 5, one of the few Netflix shows large enough to actually move the needle on its own, is being released in the second half of the year. Even with Squid Game launching just before quarter’s end, a lot of its positive impact probably spilled over into Q3 as well.

Double, Double, Toil...And Trouble?

Management also reaffirmed that it intends to “roughly double” ads revenue this year. Since they don’t tell us what ad revenue actually was last year, putting that number into context is difficult. I am not exactly down on ad revenue, but I don’t list it as a reason to be bullish, either. Most of the time I simply don’t think about ads as much as a lot of Netflix bulls do.

For one thing, if Netflix is “increasingly successful” at advertising, it’s worth remembering how much success has been redefined downwards since it launched. Netflix came out of the gate with perhaps an inflated sense of its own advertising worth, spurred no doubt by the fact in ad-free subscriptions it probably is as special as it thinks it is.

Advertising is another matter, though. While Netflix came out asking for $65 per CPM in 2022, it has more or less cut back to $30 or below since Amazon (AMZN) entered into the game right behind them. This is not so surprising when you consider the disparity not so much in size - Netflix has more than enough market cap that Amazon can’t simply outspend them in - but in data. Amazon has more data on purchase history and intent than just about any other retailer out there - with the possible exception of Costco (COST) - while Netflix has already admitted that its approach to advertising basically consists of separating audiences by age, gender, and….nope, that’s about it.

Divorces Are Expensive

This has been my argument from the beginning. Netflix simply doesn’t have the targeting data necessary to compete in advertising long term. Management has never agreed with me, however, and continues to pour capital into it. This quarter saw confirmation that Netflix’s in-house ad tech stack is now fully up and running, which means that Microsoft (MSFT) will soon be ejected from its perch atop the Netflix ad operation and become just another bidder for its ad slots.

I’m less certain than some that is something to celebrate, however. When Microsoft goes out the door, it will be taking its minimum revenue guarantee for Netflix’s ad operation with it. As with most all of Netflix’s ad operation, we don’t actually know how large this guarantee is; reports from the WSJ at the time however had it as “very large,” which is ludicrously vague but enough from a respectable publication to worry me.

Even if Netflix does double ad money this year, the Microsoft guarantee will last almost through the end of it, which means the real hit from its loss comes next year. Far from continuing to double, 2026 may see ads come to a screeching halt.

My Lingering Concerns

I am also still reticent about Netflix’s “new direction” as a whole. This is a great company, but to my mind the things that made it great the past three years are the same things that made it great the 15 before that; rigorous focus and discipline in developing a general entertainment platform that prioritizes value for money and versatility, with a strong recommendation algorithm supplementing originals production.

The general consensus has shifted, however, in favor of ads, sharing crackdowns and, most risky of all to my mind, sports. The greatest risk to the Netflix thesis is that sports will lead it astray.

The Bill Comes Due Across Town

We are seeing this happening right now at another service, in fact. The same day that Netflix reported its earnings, Comcast (CMCSA) announced that its streaming service, Peacock, would be hiking prices on its ad-supported and ad-free plans by $3 per month. That would be remarkable enough in and of itself, considering even Netflix prefers to raise prices in smaller increments than that and HBO Max - I no longer have to apologize for calling it that, since Warner Brothers (WBD) CEO David Zaslav has officially switched the name back again - still isn’t even sure it can raise prices at all.

But what makes it even more surprising is that Peacock has not exactly been storming up the charts lately. While Comcast did manage to narrowly edge out Warner Brothers on the streaming side, Peacock trails behind even Paramount’s eponymous streaming service, and Roku (ROKU) has moved ahead of it as well. Market leaders Netflix, YouTube, Amazon (AMZN) and Disney (DIS) are so far ahead Peacock’s not even in their rearview mirror.

What’s happening here? Put simply, Comcast had no choice. Ever since the NBA deal was announced, I have been a constant (overly so?) skeptic of the “good news,” insisting that the high price was such an overpayment it might compromise Peacock by requiring them to raise prices on all of Peacock’s subscribers - including the 90% of American households who do not watch the NBA - in order to pay for it.

It now appears that’s precisely what’s happening.

We've Seen This Movie Before

What is most ridiculous about this is that we’ve seen this movie before. The whole reason Netflix became one of the most valuable global media companies in existence is that American pay-TV providers regularly doubled and tripled rights payments to sports leagues, leaving non-sports subscribers with bigger and bigger bills even though their own content was not receiving any such largesse in spending.

Netflix quickly deduced that for far less money, it could buy veritably all the reruns rights of scripted American television, and a lot of movie library deals, offering non-sports subscribers a far, far better deal. The veritable exodus of such subscribers that followed is what gave rise to the phenomenon of cord-cutting.

Now, Comcast is essentially porting that flaw over to streaming. Make no mistake, it can happen again. If too much money is allocated to sports rights, the price delta between streaming services with sports and streaming services without them grows to the point that non-sports subscribers will once again migrate.

Bringing These Two Threads Together

Netflix isn’t spending enough on sports yet for this to happen to them; but what does concern me is that they might, indeed almost certainly will, go out and buy more sports. The key to Netflix’s future is avoiding falling into the same trap that Peacock is already ensnared in. So far, management is saying all the right things, and its sports bets have been suitably mild.

But the end of the Microsoft advertising floor, and the very real danger that it drives a stalling or even a reverse of ad revenue growth, may yet spur greater desperation. Sports is the primary method by which ad dollars are lured to new platforms and formats; if Netflix stock suffers from a decline in ad money pressure on management will grow to dip deeper into that pool.

This is, to my mind, the single biggest risk to Netflix; that it will abandon the playbook that got it here. The company needs to not only continue to maintain the industry leading recommendation engine that has been its bread and butter, it also needs to avoid opening a price delta with other services by overpaying on sports. Even if there are customers willing to pay $30 or more per month for sports that Netflix can acquire, Netflix needs to avoid commitments that would drive non-sports subscribers into the arms of a competitor.

Quantifying the Risk

It is difficult to put numbers on this risk because we don’t know what Netflix is getting in ad money, we don’t know how much of that is Microsoft’s minimum guarantee, and we don’t know exactly what sports deal Netflix is going to do next. So I recognize that this risk factor analysis is somewhat vague, of necessity. I believe, however, that it reflects the reality of the fundamental dynamics that will govern Netflix the next few years.

To be honest, I wish we had the Netflix of a few years ago back; considering that Netflix was already rebounding even before it launched I wonder if even management, in the quiet of their own thoughts, wishes they had the Great Netflix Correction aftermath to do over again. Was it really necessary to take the most successful media company of the 21st century and turn its non-sports, non-live, ad-free business model completely upside down? Neither Paid Sharing, nor advertising money, nor sports deals have really excited me.

Nevertheless, I would not call this quarter an empty or mere F/X-driven one. The simple fact is, Netflix continues to defy already high expectations. Even in its most mature market, UCAN, revenue growth continues apace. The second half of the year will probably be even stronger than the first, Netflix’s sports deals so far have not been detrimental - Drew Brees will join Netflix’s NFL Christmas coverage this year and the company seems determined to outshine even its Beyonce halo of 2024 - and its industry-leading recommendation algorithm is still generating healthy engagement.

Investment Summary

I took a long position in Netflix right after it crashed in 2022 and rode it all the way up to $720. I ended my long there, foolishly as it turns out. I am not entirely certain the entire run up since then is justified, but there is no denying I got out too soon.

I found myself seriously debating resuming a long position, but the lack of excitement in the market to another strong quarter suggests that just maybe the top is approaching. I am still worried about what the future of this new direction will bring - sports and ads, to my mind, do not play to Netflix’s strengths.

I cannot deny I went neutral too soon, but I am going to stay at a Hold on Netflix.

It was a great quarter, though.
