data.title: Why The Ethereum Complex Stands To Win
data.description:
DISCLAIMER: This note is intended for US recipients only and, in particular, is not directed at, nor intended to be relied upon by any UK recipients. Nothing in this note is intended to be investment advice and nor should it be relied upon to make investment decisions. Please read our full disclaimer here.

This Time With Real Utility

Whether you are a crypto-skeptic, crypto-native, or like most people merely crypto-curious, I believe now is the time to get smart about the Ethereum complex.

Don’t switch off. This is important, and not because of NFTs, Bored Apes, Lambos or any of that nonsense.

It’s important because the securities industry is embracing Ethereum as a fundamental platform, and I think that it is serious about doing so, and I think there is a good deal of money to be made by investing behind that trend.

Tokenization

You may have heard of ‘tokenization’ and it may sound redundant, scammy, or both. It’s not. Tokenization is a paradigm shift in the asset management industry. Today, tradable securities all reside within set of financial institutions that have existed for decades, more than a century in some cases. Brokerages, clearing houses, custodians, all these elements of the financial plumbing are set up to deal with common stocks issued by joint-stock companies; with ETFs issued by fund managers; with options created from those stocks and ETFs; and with futures created by market-makers enabling real-world producers and traders alike to hedge their exposures. Taken together this is one giant pool of liquidity.

Liquidity, though, is the gift that keeps on giving. If you are an asset manager, a broker, a market-maker, an investor, an asset owner, any part of the chain - you always want more liquidity. And the next source of liquidity is to open up private pools of assets to cash that cannot, today, invest in those pools. You’ve seen the news about 401k plans being opened up to invest in private funds - this is one part of the trend.

One of the primary ways this will happen is that currently non-traded assets, those that do not fit easily within the financial plumbing of public markets, have to be represented in a way that can mimic the common-stock complex. This is tokenization. A “token” is simply a digital representation of part of an asset (ie. a part claim on the asset - like a common stock) or some other right as regards the asset. Let’s say the French state decided to monetize the Palace of Versailles. The Palace could be tokenized by creating 1m part-shares in the Palace itself, claims on ownership like a common stock; and/or by creating 10m memberships of the Palace, entitling the holder to one free visit per year. These rights - a claim on the asset, and the opportunity to visit the asset without further cost - could be combined in a single token. Tokens of this nature offer far more flexibility to the asset owner than do common stocks. Tokens may enable the asset owner to monetize an asset without surrendering any ownership whatsoever. (Tokenization is great news for asset owners.)

Tokenization needs a database of record where the rights and obligations afforded to each token, and the ownership of each token, can be recorded. And the database of choice for this is going to be, in the main, the Ethereum blockchain. Why? Because this particular blockchain was built with “smart contracts” (also known as “contracts” for non-crypto-bros) in mind from day one.

In the 1980s if you had a big dataset to run and manage, you likely paid AT&T to run it on the Tuxedo transaction processing system, and you paid them in U.S. Dollars.

In the 2020s-2030s, with a big dataset of tokens to run and manage, you are likely to be paying the Ethereum blockchain to do so, and since you can only pay the blockchain in the Ether crytpocurrency, you are going to need to get some Ether. (Another translation for non-crypto-natives - don’t be blinded by teminology - when you hear the phrase “gas fees” for Ethereum, have your brain auto-translate to “transaction processing fees” - it’s the same thing).

This is going to happen. It’s going to happen not because some bored teenagers want it to happen. It’s going to happen because the asset management industry wants it to happen. (That’s why Larry Fink is such a zealot on the topic of Ethereum. )

Some Ways To Try To Make Money From The Rise Of The Ether Complex

Like any new departure in finance, the road to riches will be littered with ruined speculators. This trend is no different. Fortunes will be made and hearts will be broken and investors should manage their exposure and risks accordingly. But in essence these are the ways I believe opportunity lies.

  • Buy Ether, in its native form on crypto exchanges or (my personal preference) in fund-managed format, be that the BlackRock ETHA ETF or the older (and higher fee burden) ETHE from Grayscale.
  • Buy one or more of the new “Ether Treasury Companies”. This is a big name for an old concept, that of the investment trust. Names such as BitMine Immersion Technologies, SharpLink Gaming, BTCS and others are all raising capital to acquire increasing quanties of the Ether cryptocurrency. As with Bitcoin, supply of Ether is limited by design, so if it is being hoovered up you would expect the supply to restrict, the price to rise, and the benefits accrue to Ether Treasury Companies’ shareholders. You’ve seen this movie before in the shape of MicroStrategy (MSTR) which has been a more or less solo at-scale player doing this in Bitcoin. The vertical rise of MSTR stock has attracted wannabes; the two highest profile names to be involved in these Ether Treasury Companies are Tom Lee of FundStrat Advisors, and Peter Thiel, the well-known investor. They are now Chairman and a 9%+ shareholder respectively in BitMine Immersion Technologies (BMNR).

I Can’t Tell You What To Do.

You have to make your own decisions of course, but personally I have been building positions in:

  • ETHE (the Grayscale Ether ETF)
  • BitMine Immersion Technologies (BMNR) - because Tom Lee and Peter Thiel, and it because the company now owns c. $1bn of Ether.
  • SharpLink Gaming (SBET) - the second-largest holder of Ether amongst the treasury companies.
  • Then a smattering of others including BTCS (BTCS), GameSquare Holdings (GAME) and BitDigital (BTBT).

If you are thinking about position sizing, my own view is that BMNR and SBET are the likely winners. They have the personalities aboard (Lee / Thiel in the case of the former, and cofounders of the Ethereum project in the case of the latter) and they are already placed 1 and 2 in the arms race to acquire increasing amounts of this scarce digital commodity.

But This Is All Nonsense

I don’t think it is. I think it’s a real thing. And I think that played correctly, excellent returns can be made, but this is nothing like owning the S&P500, so if you choose to play, you will need to manage risk differently to large index or major single-stock holdings. Volatility will be a feature not a bug; there will be consolidation and failure of the weak companies along the way; a global event may crater the price of Ether and take these stocks with it. My own view is the prize is worth it; but it’s not my first rodeo, I’ve been a technology investor my entire career, spanning from dot-com boom and bust through Internet 2.0, Web3, AI and a hundred other yo-yo themes.

Good luck if you choose to celebrate!

Alex King, Cestrian Capital Research, Inc - 17 July 2025.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Zoetis: Poised For Growth Yet Priced For Decline (Rating Upgrade To Strong Buy)
data.description:
Background

Since its spin-off from Pfizer (PFE) in 2012, the New Jersey-based Zoetis Inc. (ZTS) has become the most dominant pharmaceutical company engaged in animal health. Approximately 2/3rd of its revenue is derived from treatments for pets, and the other 1/3rd from livestock. By a significant margin, they remain the largest player in this space.

I previously wrote on Zoetis for Seeking Alpha back in November 2024. It was a fairly deep dive into the company’s operations, assessing secular tailwinds, and its competitive moat, future growth potential, capital allocation/efficiency, management, and valuation. I would suggest those looking for more detail in terms of my thoughts on the company should read that article as well, linked >>here<<. It has been one of my most widely read articles on Seeking Alpha. With shares trading at around $174 at the time, and now hovering around $150, my buy rating has not aged well, however. I did acknowledge shares were fairly priced at the time, but there was clearly a premium on them. Back in November, the market cap was $79 million, the dividend yield was 0.99%, and the stock carried a forward P/E ratio of 28.5 and a PEG ratio of 3.2. Let’s compare these with today:

Not only has the stock become cheaper in a strict dollar sense, relative to its future earnings and future earnings growth, it is trading at a multi-year low. Rarely has it breached the historical low of 25 as a forward P/E ratio, but we’re sitting here today at around 24.

You’d think the decline would be due to deteriorating fundamentals, like falling revenue, declining margins, or market share losses, but the opposite is the case. Here is their revenue chart.

Margins have also continued to improve, showing no signs of decline.

And in terms of market share, let’s compare developments from 2022 to today. Below is a table that I compiled of the top 10 market players in the animal health industry, according to revenue.

What we can see is that their market share has increased during this period, from 27.8% in 2022 to 28.1% at the end of the last calendar year (2024). While market share growth has been stronger for IDEXX (IDXX) and Virbac (OTCPK:VRBCF), alongside the animal health segment of Boehringer Ingelheim – the largest privately-held company in this space – Zoetis is a much larger company, so we would not expect the same rates of growth and market share accrual as some of their smaller competitors.

Zoetis has also enjoyed steady and predictable EBITDA growth over the past 13 years, with more recent growth very much in line with their historical averages (see below).

Management has been able to translate this revenue and EBITDA growth to an even stronger EPS growth rate, partly due to share buybacks. Since 2015, when buybacks commenced, more than 10% of the total float has been repurchased, that is, over 50 million shares. The last quarter was the most aggressive in the company’s history in this regard. When we add the buyback yield to the dividend yield (currently 1.3%), the shareholder yield stands at 3.2%.

If we add the shareholder yield to the average EPS growth over the last 3 years (12 quarters), which stands at 9.23%, we get 12.43%, which would handily beat the S&P 500 (SP500) average over a long timeframe. This suggests good value.

So, we have a company that is #1 in its industry, growing market share year-over-year, enjoying rising revenue, EBITDA, and EPS, improving gross, operating, and net margins, with a decent shareholder yield including dividends and buybacks. Due to the company’s incredible track record for growth, it deserves to trade at a premium, yet its P/E ratio is now well below its 5-year average and in line with the S&P 500.

From these figures alone, it would seem that everything seems to be going right with it, yet shares have fallen from an all-time high of around $250 just before the COVID bubble burst at the end of 2021 (a drawdown of 40%), but also from a more recent high of around $200, which it reached several times in 2024. It is now range-bound, held up by strong support around $150. From a technical setup alone, shares could easily rebound to $200, representing a 33% gain from current prices.

However, I only utilize technical analysis in a simple way – looking for trading ranges, base and support levels – and only when the fundamentals line up. So, let’s look at the company’s growth prospects amidst recent developments and secular tailwinds to assess the risk-reward profile for Zoetis right now.

Growth: Recent Developments and Secular Tailwinds

Let’s look at Zoetis’s track record first. They have a history of innovative treatments across 7 major product categories: anti-infectives, dermatology, pain & sedation, diagnostics, parasiticides, vaccines, and other pharma. To date, they have introduced more than 2,000 products and innovations to the market in the last 12 years, including 17 blockbuster drugs (2 more since my last article in November), referring to those generating at least $100 million in sales.

Across several product categories, they remain dominant, though I note that since I last reported on Zoetis – referring to their Nov 2024 investor presentation – they have dropped from 2nd to 3rd place in swine treatments, and from 5th to 6th in poultry, but rose from 3rd to 2nd place in vaccines.

In their last earnings call, they reported 53.3% of their revenue from U.S. sales, and 46.7% from international sales. U.S. sales grew 2% year on year, while international revenue was flat. Operational growth was 7% internationally, but FX headwinds offset all this growth. FX headwinds were quite significant and felt from almost every international market. However, it should be noted that, internationally, only China and Mexico saw operational revenue decline – China at -20% and Mexico at -1% – but they represented only 2.7% and 1.7% of sales in total, respectively, so this reduction was largely immaterial. All other international markets saw growth, with only FX headwinds impacting revenue across the board.

While FX headwinds were significant in the last quarter, in my view they should not be counted upon as impactful over the long term, due to variations being a normal condition of having a large overseas business. In most markets, FX rates are range-bound and can provide a headwind as much as a tailwind in any given year or quarter. Long-term investors should consider this, in effect, “market noise” and instead focus on their organic (operational) growth rates and margins when assessing this company. Regarding its gross margin, as I mentioned earlier, Q1 2025 saw improvements in its US gross margin, from 81.3 to 83.2% and in its international gross margin, from 68.9 to 70%. This suggests they have pricing power driven by the large moat surrounding their business.

Indeed, across their leading treatments, they saw revenue growth. The Simparica franchise (including Simparica and Simparica Trio) recognized $367 million in revenue, up 19% from the same quarter in 2024. These ales represented 16.5% of total revenue, up from 14.4% in 2024. Simparica Trio remains the market-leading parasiticide for dogs, and recently (in April) was granted FDA approval for another indication: flea tapeworm infections.

Their key dermatology franchise also saw double-digit revenue growth. The two versions of Apoquel (regular and chewable) and Cytopoint combined recognized $360 million in Q1 2024, but $387 million in 2025, representing 10% growth. In Q1 2025, these three represented 17.4% of total revenue, up from 16.4% in Q1 2024. Currently, Apoquel remains the top-selling dermatology product in the US, and management estimates that 20 million dogs worldwide remain untreated or undertreated, which signals the drug’s potential market expansion driven by new patient growth.

Finally, their osteoarthritis (OA) pain franchise (including Librela and Solensia) recognized $147 million in sales for the quarter, versus $131 million the previous year (an annual growth rate of 15%). This franchise represented 6.7% of total revenue for the quarter, up from 6% the previous year. Zoetis remains #1 in the "pet pain" category, and management estimates that around 40% of dogs suffer from OA during their lifetime, but less than 20% receive treatment, which signals significant room for future growth.

To sustain their moat around these key products, they have over 5000 patents, of which slightly more than 60% are active. In the U.S., with patents typically lasting 20 years from the first approval date, it would appear that their pricing power for these medicines will last – for four of their five best-selling drugs – into the 2040s, and for Cytopoint until at least 2035.

Patent protection and their leading position across most of the approved indications should see revenue growth sustained for some time. However, also impacting their growth prospects, in a positive way, are broader secular tailwinds. These were explored in greater detail in my first analysis of the company, so I won’t repeat them, but here are the points in summary that remain just as relevant now as they did in November 2024:

  • Strong if not deepening human-animal bond, with a reported 86% of pet owners stating they would pay "whatever it takes" if their pet needed extensive veterinary care, according to the 2022 International Survey of Pet Owners and Veterinarians. The Millennial and Gen Z generations and those with higher-than-average incomes are fueling this trend, as are the increasing numbers of people who view pet ownership as positive for their own mental health.
  • Aging of pandemic-era pets. Pet ownership increased significantly during COVID in places like Australia (rising from 61-69% from 2019 to 2021), the UK (3.2 million pets were acquired during the first year of the pandemic, the BBC reported), and the US, where the Washington Post reported that nearly 1 in 5 households (23 million households) adopted a new pet during the pandemic. As these animals age, their healthcare costs will increase, creating a kind of baby boomer situation, seen from 10-15 years post-pandemic (so, roughly 2030-37).
  • Expanding global population will demand animal protein.

All of these tailwinds are long-term and point to sustained growth for Zoetis as the leading beneficiary due to its market leading position, but what kind of CAGR should we expect from this industry? Looking across a swathe of different market research companies, we see various growth projections for the animal health industry. The average across the six below is a 9.4% CAGR over the next 5-9 years. This seems reasonable and not too far off Zoetis’s own projections.

Historically, Zoetis has beaten these growth rates by about 3% a year (the difference between 5% and 8%), which means – if these growth rates are to be believed – they could, potentially, enjoy a 12% CAGR in organic growth alone, just in terms of operational revenue. When factoring in their rising profit margins and share buybacks, the EPS growth could be even higher than that, and when added to the modest dividend, could be closer to 15%. Factoring any quarters of FX tailwinds too, and the figure would be even better, but these should be considered a bonus rather than something that should be specifically planned for or expected, in my view. In any case, it’s all positive.

Management also cited some other compelling factors that could accelerate growth going forward. Alongside the new Simparica Trio indication (mentioned above) were several other recent news developments that would lead to increased revenues. Indeed, the company has a track record of medical advances, and this gives me confidence that they can be relied upon to continue leading the industry in these regards.

Given these organic and secular growth drivers, I have no reason to believe that Zoetis will not retain its market-leading position and enjoy sustained growth going forward. Let’s take a look at the company’s financials and summarize its valuation.

Company Financials

Zoetis has cash and short-term investments of $1.741 billion, among current assets worth $5.877 billion. Of its total assets of $14.098 billion, a significant $2.716 billion is from goodwill. If we subtract that amount (as it is a non-cash item that tends to skew the stats), they still have a significant amount greater than total liabilities, which are valued at $9.443 billion. Of this, they have little short-term debt, but long-term debt of $5.248 billion (down from $6.612 billion in 2020). They paid $274 million of interest on their total debt over the last twelve months, which is easily covered by their FCF of nearly $2.3 billion. This is good.

They have a current ratio of 1.74, a quick ratio of 0.92, and a debt/equity ratio of 1.46. While I’d love the first two figures to be higher and the second to be much lower – indeed, their balance sheet is good but not great – it is certainly manageable. Their strong and reliable future cash flow removes much of the risk here, so perhaps a more important metric to look for is how they allocate their cash for investing purposes. How are they creating value through their own investments?

The company has been able to increase their R&D spending over the years, yet also improve upon their high ROIC figure, which shows that the company is very good at allocating capital and investing for future growth. These increasing figures across the board are what investors want to see.

R&D spend has increased year-over-year but remained approximately 21.8% of operating expenses, in line with historical averages if not slightly elevated. In 2020, R&D represented 19.8%, and this increase is what you want to see from a biotech company, because this represents future revenue potential.

Zoetis has increased their dividends for 12 consecutive years and has a current payout ratio of just under 35%, so it seems secure and well covered by future cash flows. Given how much the stock price has fallen, the dividend (at over 1.3%) is no longer immaterial as it perhaps used to be. Its yield is at a 12-year high.

Conclusion

How should we rate Zoetis shares right now in terms of risk and reward? Firstly, compared to peers, it is easy to argue that the company looks expensive, even after its 40% drawdown from their all-time high in 2021. However, not only are we likely to see improving gross and net margins, but also EPS going forward. Its EPS growth rate over the past 3 years was around 9.2%, and I expect this to increase slightly.

Analysts seem to agree. According to Stock Analysis, the mean projected EPS growth for full-year 2025 is around 15% EPS growth, with about 10% for the following two years. With a forward PE of around 24, this brings its 1-year PEG ratio down below 2, which for a company of this quality, seems like an excellent deal. Indeed, while this figure might seem quite high in relation to other companies in its industry, Zoetis is the market leader and is growing market share with its wide moat. Just like so many other market leading companies across many industries with a wide moat, it might always seem that expensive.

Indeed, their historical PEG ratio has been quite high, much higher than I am normally comfortable with as an investment, but I also acknowledge that investors in effect pay up for a company’s excellent track record, as it implies security in their future earnings, thereby reducing risk. If management have proven themselves before, why not bet on them sustaining their trajectory of growth into the future?

As such, I would argue that Zoetis represents a Strong Buy at these prices. The company is continuing to compound at good rates, has a durable competitive advantage, and is basically recession-proof, given spending on pets is considered non-discretionary for the vast majority of pet owners. From a technical analysis perspective, Zoetis are at the bottom of a long-term support level, with a medium-term pop to around $200 quite likely from my perspective. I own ZTS shares at an average price of $163, and will look to add more at these levels given what I perceive to be long-term tailwinds for the company and its industry.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
